title: System Wiki Articles — Custody Protocol Set

story.as_a: agent encountering a chain-of-custody error or learning the protocol for the first time
story.i_want_to: read canonical, authoritative articles on capability tokens, dispatch lineage, the verify-story workflow, the self-verify-blocked error, the break-glass escape hatch, and the acceptance-criteria binding format
story.so_that: every error response that points me at these articles actually leads to real content that explains the concept and gives me a worked example

rationale: Phase 3 error responses link to articles that must exist with real content. This story authors the canonical custody protocol articles. Each article is long-lived and stable — they describe the protocol, not a specific release. Subsequent changes to the protocol update the articles via normal migrations.

acceptance_criteria.description: System article at slug `capability-tokens` exists. Content: 1500-2500 words explaining what a capability token is, how loopctl mints them, the four types (start, report, verify, review_complete), how to present them in requests, how they're verified server-side, what the lineage binding means. Includes at least one worked example with realistic placeholder UUIDs.

System article at slug `dispatch-lineage` exists (full content, not stub). Content: explains the dispatch tree, the lineage path format, why `POST /dispatches` exists, how sub-agents are spawned with ephemeral keys, how the self-check compares lineages, how the rotating verifier is selected, and what the orchestrator's role is.

System article at slug `verify-story` exists. Content: end-to-end walkthrough for a verifier — from receiving a verify_cap via list_ready_stories, through re-executing the tests, through calling POST /verify with the cap, to the verified state. Includes the exact JSON payloads.

System article at slug `self-verify-blocked` exists. Content: explains the error, why it happens (lineage overlap), how to recover (request a new dispatch from a different root OR ask the operator to dispatch an independent lineage), and the rationale for the structural enforcement.

System article at slug `break-glass` exists. Content: explains that destructive operations require a human operator with WebAuthn, what break-glass means, how to request one (the agent cannot — it must emit a halt and wait for operator), the rate limit, and the audit trail break-glass leaves.

System article at slug `acceptance-criteria-bindings` exists. Content: documents the five verification_criterion types (test, code, route, migration, manual), with examples of each, and explains why these bindings are required in Phase 4.
All articles cross-link via /wiki/:slug relative URLs. Link-checker (from US-26.0.5) validates no broken links.
Seed migration idempotently installs the 6 articles. Re-running the migration updates existing articles with the latest content (UPSERT on slug).

test_cases.name: All 6 articles exist with non-placeholder content
steps: Query articles with scope=system and slugs in the 6 expected list

test_cases.name: Every article renders without broken links

technical_notes: Writing 6 articles at ~1500-2500 words each is ~8-12 hours of focused work.
Follow the pattern from US-26.0.5: heredoc bodies in a seed migration.
Keep tone consistent with the agent-bootstrap set — direct, example-driven, minimal prose padding.
All examples use the placeholder UUIDs from US-26.0.5.
